channel.title: noahs md notes
channel.generator: Webpage HTML Export plugin for Obsidian

item.title: Glossary
item.description:
Glossary for Microelectronic Systems links will go to relevant note (imo) ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter): A peripheral that converts continuous real-world analog voltages into discrete digital binary numbers.
Address Bus: Wires used by the MCU to specify the physical memory address it wants to read from or write to.
ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit): The part of the CPU core responsible for performing mathematical and logical operations.
Anti-aliasing Filter: An analog low-pass filter placed before an ADC to physically block frequencies higher than the Nyquist limit, preventing digitized signal distortion.
Asynchronous: Serial communication that does not use a shared clock line. Both devices must pre-agree on a speed (Baud rate).
Baud Rate: The number of symbol or signal level changes per second in a communication channel.
Bit Rate: The number of individual binary bits transmitted per second.
Bus: A communication system that transfers data between components inside the MCU (Data, Address, and Peripheral buses).
Chip Select (CS): A dedicated control wire in SPI used by the Master to wake up and communicate with one specific Slave device.
Context Saving: The process where the MCU pauses the main program and pushes its current state onto the stack before executing an ISR.
DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter): A peripheral that converts discrete digital binary numbers into a continuous analog voltage.
Data Bus: Wires that carry the actual data being processed around the MCU.
Duty Cycle: The percentage of time a PWM signal is in the active (HIGH) state during a single period.
Embedded System: A dedicated computer system designed for a specific control function within a larger mechanical or electrical device.
Floating Pin: A digital input pin that is not connected to a definite logic voltage (leaving it susceptible to random noise).
Flyback Diode: A diode placed in reverse-parallel with an inductive load (like a motor) to safely dissipate inductive kickback voltage and protect the MCU.
Full-Duplex: Communication where data can be sent and received simultaneously (e.g., SPI, UART).
Half-Duplex: Communication where data can flow in both directions, but only one direction at a time (e.g., I2C).
Harvard Architecture: A computer architecture with separate, independent memory spaces and buses for data and instructions.
I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit): A 2-wire, half-duplex, synchronous serial protocol that uses embedded addressing instead of physical Chip Select lines.
Inductive Kickback: A massive, potentially damaging voltage spike created when the current flowing through an inductor (like a DC motor) is suddenly turned off.
ISR (Interrupt Service Routine): A specialized block of code that executes immediately when a hardware interrupt is triggered.
LSB (Least Significant Bit): The bit in a binary number with the lowest mathematical value. In UART, this is typically transmitted first.
MCU (Microcontroller Unit): A small computer on a single integrated circuit containing a processor core, memory, and programmable I/O peripherals.
MSB (Most Significant Bit): The bit in a binary number with the highest mathematical value. In SPI and I2C, this is typically transmitted first.
Nyquist Theorem: A rule stating that to accurately digitize a signal, you must sample it at least twice as fast as its highest frequency component.
Parity Bit: An extra bit added to the end of a data frame for simple error checking (e.g., Even Parity ensures the total number of 1s in the frame is an even number).
Polling (Software): A technique where the MCU continuously checks the status of an input device over and over in a while loop.
Pull-up / Pull-down Resistor: A resistor used to tie a floating pin to a known logic level (HIGH or LOW) when a mechanical switch is open.
Pulse Width: The absolute time (usually in milliseconds or microseconds) that a PWM signal is HIGH during one period.
PWM (Pulse Width Modulation): A highly efficient technique to simulate an analog voltage by rapidly switching a digital transistor ON and OFF.
Quantisation Error: The physical difference between the actual continuous analog input value and its digitized "staircase" representation (Max error = LSB).
RAM (SRAM): Volatile memory used to store temporary runtime variables. Data is lost when power is removed.
Resolution: The smallest physical change in an analog signal that a converter can measure or output (Formula: ).
ROM / Flash: Non-volatile memory used to store the compiled program code. Data remains when power is removed.
SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface): A 4-wire, full-duplex, synchronous serial protocol.
Synchronous: Communication that relies on a shared physical Clock line to coordinate data transfer timing.
UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter): A hardware device/protocol that handles asynchronous serial communication using just TX and RX wires.
Von Neumann Architecture: A computer architecture that uses a single, shared memory space and a single bus for both data and instructions.

item.title: Formula Sheet & Exam Index
item.description:
Current Limiting Resistor: Note: If LEDs are in parallel, the total current is . Period (): Pulse Width: Average Voltage: Total States: Resolution (Smallest measureable change): Max Digital Output Range: Quantisation Error: (Half of the resolution) Digital Output Value (ADC): (Truncate/round down to nearest whole number) Link Efficiency: *(See UART & Async Comms or I2C Protocol) Bit Rate vs Baud Rate: Max Decimal Value: Resolution of Base- ADC:
Q1: ADC & DAC calculations (Resolution, range, converting binary to analog).
Q2: Digital I/O (Calculating LED resistors, parallel LEDs, potentiometers).
Q3: PWM & SPI (Calculating minimum sampling freq, minimum clock freq for SPI frames).
Q4: Trinary (Base-3) number system conversions & ADC calculations.
Q1: Embedded Systems (Smart Fridge design).
Q2: ADC & DAC & PWM (Smart watch heart rate sensor, Nyquist limit).
Q3: Digital I/O (Connecting switches, LEDs, and motor protection/flyback diodes).
Q4: I2C Protocol (Sketching Data & Clock timing diagrams for address 0x9).
Q1: Embedded Systems (Smart Vending Machine).
Q2: Quadinary (Base-4) number system conversions.
Q3: Digital I/O (More LED and motor PWM calculations).
Q4: SPI Protocol (Master/6-Slaves, sketching clock/data diagrams for 0xB3).
Q1: Embedded Systems (Smart Vacuum Cleaner).
Q2: Pental (Base-5) number system conversions.
Q3: PWM & LEDs (Using an I-V curve to find LED voltage/current, motor circuits).
Q4: I2C Protocol (Sketching diagrams for address 0xA, addressing limits).

item.title: Number Systems & Custom Bases
item.description:
🔗 External Reference: Base Converter ToolExam papers love to invent imaginary bases (Trinary base-3, Quadinary base-4, Pental base-5).Basic Conversions: For a Base- system, place values are ... Example (Base-5 "Pental" number 42): .Max Values: The max decimal value of an -digit Base- system is .ADC Calculations in Custom Bases!
They will combine this with ADC & DAC. If you have a 6-pent ADC with a 5V reference: Total states = Resolution =

item.title: UART & Async Comms
item.description:
Type: Asynchronous, Full-Duplex. Wires: TX (Transmit), RX (Receive). No clock wire! Open: Pasted image 20260430202655.png
The Frame (Sketching rules): Idle state: Always HIGH. Start bit: Always drops LOW to wake up the receiver. Data bits: Usually 8 bits. Sent LSB (Least Significant Bit) First! Parity bit: Error checking. "Even parity" means the total number of 1s in the data+parity must be an even number. Stop bit: Always HIGH. Returns to idle.
Open: Pasted image 20260430202705.png
Efficiency Example: Sending 1 byte (8 bits) with 1 Start, 1 Parity, and 1 Stop bit = 11 bits total. Efficiency = .

item.title: 0bec019e67da2b7b5a6e589eb96945e5_MD5
dfdf766c2b268c48fd7ad66b85c31b8c_MD5
I2C Protocol
item.description:
Type: Synchronous, Half-Duplex. Wires (2): SDA (Data), SCL (Clock). Open: Pasted image 20260430202431.png
How it addresses devices:
Unlike SPI Protocol, I2C doesn't use Chip Select lines. The first byte sent over SDA is the Address. Highly scalable (connect 50+ sensors on 2 wires).The Protocol Frame: START: SDA goes LOW while SCL is HIGH. Address: (Usually 7-bit + 1 R/W bit). ACK/NACK: Receiver pulls line low. Data bits: MSB first. Data must only change when Clock is LOW. STOP: SDA goes HIGH while SCL is HIGH.
Open: Pasted image 20260430202507.png
To avoid false marker detection, there is a minimum delay between the SCL falling edge and changing SDA, and between changing SDA and the SCL rising edge. The minimum delay time is dependent upon the data transfer rate in use. Note that an I2C message containing n data bits (including acknowledgements) contains n + 1 clock pulses.

item.title: d6cb4f080f39a4b68a54d57e83c182f1_MD5
b4c08087f02dfca9042b8d849210a3c8_MD5
SPI Protocol
item.description:
Type: Synchronous, Full-Duplex. Wires (4): MOSI, MISO, SCK (Clock), CS (Chip Select). Open: Pasted image 20260430201945.png
The CS (Chip Select) Line: Because SPI doesn't send addresses in the data, the Master needs a physical wire (CS) going to every single slave. Pulling a specific CS line LOW wakes up that specific slave.
Constraint: The number of peripherals is limited by the number of physical digital output pins available on the MCU.
This animation shows a scenario where the master sends 8-bit data and simultaneously reads 16-bit data from the slave. The lower boxes represent the device's memories.

item.title: Serial_Peripheral_Interface_Animation
Pasted image 20260430202011
1ddaaee0ced236cc0b2bd217c8c4d177_MD5
ADC & DAC
item.description:
Converting between real-world analog signals and digital binary.Open: Pasted image 20260430201749.png
Formulas (for -bit converters): Total States: Resolution: (This is the smallest measurable change). Max Digital Output Range: Quantisation Error: (Half the resolution). Sampling & Filters: Nyquist Theorem: Sample at least twice as fast as the highest frequency in your signal. Anti-aliasing Filter: An analog low-pass filter placed before the ADC to block frequencies higher than Nyquist.
(Exam trick: What if the ADC isn't binary? See Number Systems & Custom Bases)

item.title: Serial Communication Basics
item.description:
Sending data one bit at a time over a wire. Synchronous vs. Asynchronous: Sync: Uses a shared Clock line (e.g., SPI Protocol, I2C Protocol). Good for fast, on-board chips.
Async: No clock line (e.g., UART & Async Comms). Ends must pre-agree on a speed. Good for long distances. Bit Rate vs. Baud Rate: Bit rate: Number of binary bits per second. Baud rate: Number of symbol changes per second. If a signal has 4 voltage levels (00, 01, 10, 11), one symbol (baud) = 2 bits. Therefore, Bit Rate = 2 Baud Rate.

item.title: Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)
item.description:
A digital way to simulate an analog voltage, highly useful for driving motors or dimming LEDs.Open: Pasted image 20260430201606.png
Key Metrics: Period (): Duty Cycle: Percentage of time the signal is HIGH. Pulse Width: Average Output Voltage: Why PWM over Analog? (Efficiency) Analog circuits act like resistors and burn power as heat (). PWM uses transistors acting as pure digital switches. They have low ON-resistance and infinite OFF-resistance, meaning almost zero power is wasted. Power is only lost during the very fast switching transition.

item.title: f117559a43bf1ba5c7c5fc49dd695779_MD5
Digital IO and Protection
item.description:
🔗 External Reference: Official STM32 Nucleo Pinout (Mbed)1. Switch Interfacing (Digital In) Floating Pins: An open switch leaves a pin "floating" (unknown state). Pull-up / Pull-down Resistors: Usually ~10k. Ensures the pin is tied to Logic 1 (3.3V) or Logic 0 (0V) when the switch is open.
Open: Pasted image 20260430201449.png
2. LED Interfacing (Digital Out) Current Limiting Resistors: Prevents blowing the LED. Formula: 3. Motor Interfacing (High Power Out) Isolation: Never connect a motor directly to a GPIO. Use a MOSFET. Inductive Kickback: Motors are inductors. When turned off, they create a massive voltage spike. Always draw a Flyback Diode in parallel with the motor to protect the MCU.
Driven using Pulse Width Modulation (PWM).

item.title: b265b6a190f8a82b95446fed8a58ef52_MD5
dc7465dd96e0f3d0f49a7589edaf5c30_MD5
Task Control
item.description: How does the MCU know when an input happens? How it works: A continuous loop while(1) regularly checks the input. Pros: Simple to write. Cons: Wastes power. Inefficient for rare events (e.g., a lift button). How it works: Hardware triggers the MCU only when an event occurs. The MCU can sleep otherwise. ISR (Interrupt Service Routine): The code that runs when the interrupt fires. Must be fast! Context Saving: The MCU saves its state to the stack, runs the ISR, and loads the state back.

item.title: Microcontroller Architecture
item.description:
Harvard vs. Von Neumann: Von Neumann: Shared memory and bus for both data and instructions. Harvard: Separate memory spaces and buses for data (variables) and instructions (program). Allows simultaneous reading of data and instructions. Open: Pasted image 20260430200339.png
Core Components: ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit): Performs mathematical and logical operations. Core Registers: Hold values for the ALU and report CPU status. Clock: Synchronizes all parts of the MCU. Buses: Data Bus: Carries the actual data around the MCU. Address Bus: Holds the memory location address. (Max space = , e.g., 32-bit bus = 4GB).
Peripheral Bus: Where I/O hardware is connected (links to Digital IO and Protection). Memory: RAM (SRAM): Volatile. Used to store runtime variables. ROM/Flash: Non-volatile. Used to store the compiled program and static peripheral addresses.

item.title: 9482c933f3eb1df69d58a08f3187489f_MD5
Embedded Systems
item.description:
Definition: A computer system with a dedicated function within a larger mechanical or electrical system, often with real-time computing constraints. Unlike a general-purpose PC, it is designed for a specific task.Exam Examples to know: Washing Machine: Inputs (buttons, water temp/level sensors, weight sensors). Outputs (LCD, motor PWM, water valves). Smart Fridge: Adds connectivity (Bluetooth/WiFi UART), touchscreens. Vending Machine: Inputs (Card reader, keypad). Outputs (Motor to drop item, screen).
Key Controller: Relies on a Microcontroller (MCU) to process inputs and drive outputs.
